podcasts.name: Unsupervised Learning
podcasts.title: Ep 88: Unpacking DeepMind's Quest for SuperIntelligence with Demis Hassabis' Biographer
podcasts.transcript:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:05
Demis has a Nobel Prize. Sam didn't finish his first degree. Therefore, Demis doesn't take Sam very seriously.

Speaker 2 | 00:05 - 00:09
Do you think he feels like or or will have to become more of a public figure than he is today?

Speaker 1 | 00:09 - 00:12
Demis, in some ways, is too sensible.

Speaker 2 | 00:12 - 00:15
What was the moment, I guess, your view of him kind of shifted the most?

Speaker 1 | 00:15 - 00:28
He would sometimes erupt in these conversations I was having, and he would start banging the table and saying, maybe if we approach science the right way, we understand more about nature, we will be getting closer to something that we could perhaps call God.

Speaker 2 | 00:28 - 01:02
Sebastian Malaby spent over thirty hours with DeepMind CEO and cofounder, Demis Esabas, in preparation to write his book, The Infinity Machine. I got to sit down with Sebastian to get all his reflections on this experience. It was fascinating to talk about some of the stories that Sebastian reported on in this book, including the fact that Reid Hoffman offered a billion dollars to Demis to spin DeepMind out of Google at some point. We talked about Demis' relationship with Elon and how that's evolved over the years, as well as what he thinks of Sam, Dario, and the general relationship between all the AI leaders in the space today. And we hit on Sebastian's reflections on Demis, what motivates him, what his blind spots are.

Speaker 2 | 01:02 - 01:20
Just a fascinating conversation with someone who's spent unprecedented time with someone who's really, at the cutting edge of AI today. I think folks will really enjoy Sebastian's candid takes here. Without further ado, here he is. Well, Sebastian, thanks so much for coming on the podcast. Really appreciate it.

Speaker 1 | 01:20 - 01:21
Thank you. It's great to be with you.

Speaker 2 | 01:21 - 01:42
I feel like the Infinity Machine and your kind of story of DeepMind and Demis Esabas has captivated a bunch of folks in the AI world. I know that I inhaled this book. I think I finished it in, like, twenty four hours. It's just a a gripping story, and you got this just incredible access to Demis. I think, you know, the sitting in this pub for for multi hours at a time, talking about all sorts of things.

Speaker 2 | 01:42 - 02:16
Just just a fascinating, you know, opportunity and window into to definitely one of the most interesting people and and companies in this AI race today. And so appreciate you writing the book and appreciate you coming on the podcast to talk about So our goal today is to, you know, use your reporting to understand the people, decisions, and dynamics that shape the AI race that we're living through. And so maybe to start, I feel like one of the interesting themes in your book is whether this race that we have today between the labs was inevitable or could have gone differently, if different players or decisions had had been done. I guess I'm curious. After doing this work, do you think there was another path, or or was this kind of inevitable?

Speaker 1 | 02:16 - 02:40
You know, I think it was inevitable. I think when you have this sort of supremely strong technology, there's gonna be multiple labs in multiple countries that are just desperate to try and build it. And we know the China stack, you know, is pretty strong. And so despite the lack of semiconductors, they were gonna have a go at it and they are actually doing pretty well. And then clearly in The US, and then in a few other countries, you've got the Austral in France, Coher in Canada.

Speaker 1 | 02:40 - 02:58
You know, there was bound to be many players just as it's the technology is too sweet for it to be only interesting to one team. What's strange about this whole debate though is that's not how people saw it ex ante, that, you know, when Demis was starting DeepMind, he really hoped that he could avoid the race dynamic. It seems naive in retrospect, but that's what he hoped.

Speaker 2 | 02:58 - 03:23
One really compelling anecdote you had was I I believe Demis in his interviews would say, you know, he he was interviewing candidate, and he'd say, look. At some point, we may be really close to AGI, and we'll go, fly to a bunker and and and figure this all out as kind of like one team. Would you be willing to, you know, get on that flight and and and do that? You know, think certainly as part of the the founding origin of Anthropic is also this belief like, hey. We need to be the team that, like, gets you know, as we get to the precipice of AGI, we're the ones figuring this out.

Speaker 2 | 03:24 - 03:33
You know, clearly, a a key part of starting all these companies. Do you think these folks, I mean, particularly, Demis, like, do they still believe this, or or how is their kind of thinking evolved having seen the way things have played out these past years?

Speaker 1 | 03:33 - 03:57
No. I think Demis has swung from one extreme to the other. You know, he began by thinking there could be a singleton scenario, just one lab. And by that, he really meant deep mind in himself. To the opposite extreme where he now sees that there's a very crowded field and therefore that it's almost pointless for one lab to pursue safety by itself because if one lab is safe and then the other ones aren't, it doesn't make the world safer.

Speaker 1 | 03:57 - 04:03
So he really has shifted to seeing this as a collective action problem that only a government can solve.

Speaker 2 | 04:03 - 04:30
Because one thing I I thought was really compelling for your book was, obviously, there was this early AI safety summit that Demis and the and the team put together, and they're sharing, you know, the progress they've made and and why they're worried about it. And, know, you some people in the room like Reid Hoffman and Elon Musk take that information, and then they're like, oh, this technology really is starting to work. We should ourselves potentially do something around that. You know, do you think, obviously, to to get safety to work going forward, you need these different companies to to agree to share things and and collaborate. I imagine that left a bad taste in in in Demis' mouth.

Speaker 2 | 04:30 - 04:38
How do you think he and and other kind of key players in this space think about, you know, potentially sharing what they're doing with with given that, you know, past experience?

Speaker 1 | 04:38 - 04:49
Yeah. I mean, you're totally right. That was in 2015, summer of twenty fifteen. They have this summit SpaceX. Elon Musk is hosting it, and the idea is he's gonna be brought into the tent by DeepMind.

Speaker 1 | 04:49 - 05:07
He's gonna be part of their efforts. He's going to be, you know, chairing this sort of safety oversight board, And therefore, he wouldn't set up a competitor. And, of course, at the end of 2015, he did set up a competitor, OpenAI. And so that kind of really drove home the reality that we're gonna have a race. And I think if you ask now, okay.

Speaker 1 | 05:07 - 05:24
So what about future collaboration? I think from Tembus' point of view or from any of the lab leaders, frankly, you can't trust the other guys. And therefore, the only way you get trust is if you have a government enforcer that comes along and say, look. Here's the rules for everybody. There's gonna be a level playing field.

Speaker 1 | 05:24 - 05:46
You're all gonna have to, you know, abide by some sort of safety, slowdown, you know, pretesting of models before you release them, all that stuff. And you will have to do it. And then the reaction, of course, will be, yeah. But what about the guys in China? And that's why, ultimately, I think this has to be a US China collaboration, you know, remote though that prospect may seem to many listeners right now.

Speaker 2 | 05:46 - 06:07
I mean, do you think, Demis and others, I think that's actually realistic? I mean, obviously, there there's it you know, it's it's such a a pressing problem, but, you know, it doesn't seem like, folks have the most confidence in in governments and certainly, you know, inter government, collaboration across the world. Like, it it intellectually makes sense that that is kind of the the clear way to solve this problem. But do you think they they think there's actually a probability this this does happen successfully?

Speaker 1 | 06:07 - 06:47
Well, you know, I think they ought to believe that it could happen. And we we do have the Food and Drug Administration and people complain that it's slow and all that, but but it does, you know, have expert reviewers who look at clinical trials and say is this drug safe or not and can it be released. And if you can do that for pharmaceuticals and you should do it, AI models that can be more destructive on a grander scale than a drug certainly need to be reviewed ex ante. And I think in Britain actually you have an example of a government AI safety institute that's pretty effective, has some high class scientists. I mean the chief scientist there is Geoffrey Irving who was at DeepMind originally did a lot of the post training on the early models.

Speaker 1 | 06:47 - 07:14
He's a really serious researcher. Was also at OpenAI by the way and a close collaborator of Darius. So you've got serious people there and they have found vulnerabilities in systems in the past and quietly shared them with the private labs that have failed to find the same vulnerabilities. So it shows that you can aggregate some technical expertise inside these public institutions because a certain number of folk feel motivated and public spirited enough to go work for the government. So I don't think we should give up on that.

Speaker 2 | 07:14 - 07:35
From the outside, it's hard to tell. You know, it it's almost like such a dispiriting story of, you know, Demis kinda being idealistic and then realizing this dynamic isn't gonna play out. And so it's hard to tell, you know, I guess, reading between the lines in your book, whether he's kind of just resigned to this dynamic or is is found newfound hope in in, like, the the actual possibility of, you know, intergovernment collaboration? Because, obviously, even the examples you give are just within a single country. Right?

Speaker 2 | 07:35 - 07:39
Certainly not at the scale of of of coordination across countries.

Speaker 1 | 07:39 - 07:55
Yeah. I mean, what he what he says, I mean, the very last line of my book is, I'm optimistic still, and that's a quote from Demis trying to, you know, persuade me. I've been I'm pushing him in in in my interviews with him. Are you really optimistic? Because it seems like we've got a race dynamic, you know, in, of course, January 2025.

Speaker 1 | 07:55 - 08:13
You have deep sea come out, and that really signals the advent of the Chinese labs into this space. And, you know, I'm writing through 2025 into the end of twenty twenty five, and I'm saying, you still optimistic, Demet? And he says, yes. I'm optimistic still. And I think the reason for that insistence apart from like, what what's he gonna say?

Speaker 1 | 08:14 - 08:43
It's also that, you know, when something kind of dramatic happens, the world does coalesce and take action. You know, if you look at COVID, the reaction to COVID, I mean, government on a national basis really did stuff that people didn't expect beforehand. You know? So in a crisis, people do react. When you have something like, you know, trade where protectionism has a pretty slow and subtle effect on economic performance, you don't really get a political reaction to try and fix that.

Speaker 1 | 08:43 - 09:05
And that's what we've seen in the last few years. But when you when it if if you have a acute thing like mythos, an anthropics model comes out, you know, and people suddenly get worried, You see the US government pretty much do a one eighty from a less affair position to, oh, well, we better control this kind of position. And I think that shows the shock effect is extremely important in determining whether you get government action or not.

Speaker 2 | 09:05 - 09:37
Switching gears, obviously, a key part of, you know, and through line through your your whole work is just the story of of DeepMind and its ultimate relationship with Google, and it's just an incredible story. And so I guess, as I recall, you started this or you kinda got Demis to agree to this, like, right before ChatGPT. And so, obviously, post AlphaFold and some really interesting things, but certainly the world, evolved in a bunch of interesting ways from the start of your project. People talk about Demis and DeepMind all the time. You know, before you published this book, but you've kinda done the research, what do you think the popular narrative got, like, most wrong about Demees, DeepMind, the way people would talk about, you know, the the two?

Speaker 1 | 09:37 - 09:59
Well, the extraordinary thing to me is how people basically discounted Demees. I mean, you know, I I've been interviewed a bunch of times since my book came out a few weeks ago and, you know, often people can't pronounce his name. They say Demise and they say Hasabis instead of Hasabis. They kind of barely know who he is. You know my publisher Penguin Press was figuring out what to do the how to do the cover design.

Speaker 1 | 09:59 - 10:08
And they wanted to use his picture but at the same time they didn't think people would recognize him. So you couldn't sell books based on some, you know, person that he recognized. Right.

Speaker 2 | 10:08 - 10:11
At this point, I assume Sam, Dario, they'd be fine for for a cover of a book.

Speaker 1 | 10:11 - 10:12
Yeah. Yeah. Totally.

Speaker 2 | 10:12 - 10:13
Is it Elon. Yeah.

Speaker 1 | 10:13 - 10:41
Elon, for sure. And and Demis, you know, is the guy who founded the OG lab in 2010 way before anybody else really created the model that then OpenAI copied later. I remember going to see Dario when I was doing my research and, you know, he said, yes, Demis was the original figure and he's got the whole AI for science space to himself which was true at the time. I think it's less true now. But so I think people just underestimated how important he he is.

Speaker 1 | 10:42 - 11:01
They underestimated Google as a company because they thought Innovator's Dilemma, they were too slow. OpenAI went fast with a model with a with a chatbot. And now they've got this massive brand effect, OpenAI does. And so nobody can catch up. That proved wrong.

Speaker 1 | 11:01 - 11:27
And you know, as of late twenty twenty five, the Google DeepMind models, Gemini three point o were better on the leaderboards than the adversaries. Of course, since then we've seen a big anthropic surge. But you know, the the point is I think people were too quick to crown OpenAI and Sam Altman as the winner and underestimated both Demis as a person and Google DeepMind as a company.

Speaker 2 | 11:27 - 12:01
Is interesting, obviously, like, since you're reporting, you know, I guess in 2026, you've had these rise of these, you know, coding agents and, you know, it's certainly, you know, Anthropic and Cloud Code were the first there and OpenAI, you know, with with Codex was was right behind. It does feel like Google continues to do, like, amazing science and, you know, they do you know, they always do well on the leaderboards. But for whatever reason, it it struggles in, like, the zeitgeist and, like, actual usage on the product side. If these two monumental moments were, you know, ChatGPT and consumer products and then ClaudeCode and and and the coding agents, Google doesn't really have a a super competitive product in either of those spaces just from a usage perspective. Not the products themselves are are good.

Speaker 2 | 12:01 - 12:06
The models are good, but they haven't really figured out the the latter part, and I'm wondering kinda what you make of that.

Speaker 1 | 12:06 - 12:29
People use Gemini more than we realize. Right? You know, it's sort of bundled into Google search at this point, the AI mode thing. But I I I take the broader point, which is that both of these sort of seminal moments in proving out, you know, the the the consumer experience, which were ChatTPT, as you say, on the consumer side and then more recently with coding. Neither of those came from Google DeepMind.

Speaker 1 | 12:29 - 12:58
And I think what that perhaps shows us is that, you know, partly because of Demis's personality and his intellectual formation, which was PhD in neuroscience, this very broad study of what intelligence might be, a very broad approach therefore to building artificial intelligence. There's this there's this kind of let's try everything approach to AI research. Whenever there's like two different paths you could go down, they say, well, we'll do both. And if we can find the third path, we'll probably do that too. They're very hedged.

Speaker 1 | 12:58 - 13:27
Right? Whereas I think Anthropic got to coding because it was willing to take a more concentrated bet. It never went into the whole field of, you know, generative video, just never had a sort of equivalent. And, you know, at the same time, OpenAI, being a startup, didn't have the reputational baggage that it had to protect that Google has, and so it willing to put out a chatbot that hallucinated a lot at the beginning.

Speaker 2 | 13:27 - 13:52
It honestly has a lot of echoes of of the transformer itself. Right? And and, basically, I think OpenAI making the bet of going all in on on scaling transformers, which were obviously invented at Google and Google pursuing a bunch of different paths. And then to your point, OpenAI starting to do a bunch of things and Anthropic deciding, hey, we're just focusing on coding. Have you seen kind of a shift in in and DeepMind's focus, I guess, what's played out and given this kind of, it's such a tension, right?

Speaker 2 | 13:52 - 14:13
As a polymath, as someone who's so interested in many facets of AI to focus on a ton of different things versus the thing that's happening at the moment. Obviously, OpenAI, I think famously has kind of consolidated down and said we were doing too much. Now we've got to really hone in and catch up to Anthropicana coding models. How do you think Demis and the DeepMind folks think about this at this

Speaker 1 | 14:13 - 14:31
I think, you know, they do have this tendency to try and do everything. As I was saying, I don't think that's been cured. I mean, you know, I was chatting actually just last week with one of the young scientists from that from that shop. And, you know, that was very much the tenor of what he was telling me. It's like, you know, we always make multiple bets.

Speaker 1 | 14:31 - 14:58
We never really go hard down one avenue. And and, you know, from a corporate point of view, maybe this makes sense. I mean, if we if we think about what happened with the consumer side, the sort of general chatbot model, yeah, they were late. They were late both in trying to develop it after the transformer thing dropped in 2017, and then they were late again in productizing, you know, behind ChatTPT. But then they caught up by 2025.

Speaker 1 | 14:58 - 15:15
So maybe if you have very deep pockets like Google and a very deep technical bench and tons of human talent and amazing amounts of compute, you know, you can afford you're they're not doing Apple. Right? Apple is the absolute extreme where they just say, whatever. We're not gonna do this AI thing. We'll just charge people to put it on our iPhone.

Speaker 1 | 15:16 - 15:26
That's an extremely hands off position towards AI. Google is much more in it, but it doesn't mind being a couple of years behind because it figures it can catch up.

Speaker 2 | 15:26 - 15:56
And I wonder at some point whether that that does shift. Obviously, you've you've, you know, allowed $2,000,000,000,000, plus. We'll see what they're end up being worth companies to to or or at least in the in the prediction market seem to be worth that, you know, come come up, and and and gain a lot of territory. And, you know, think I it's it's it'll be really interesting to see going forward, you know, whether that that focused or or that, like, diversity of different things they're pursuing, you know, ends up ends up continuing. You know, you talk a lot about in the book about, you know, the governance of DeepMind and Google and, like, the way these two organizations work together.

Speaker 2 | 15:57 - 16:11
And And I think actually one of the most interesting parts I thought was this DeepMind was considering spinning out of Google. There was, like, some financing behind that. They ultimately chose not to. Why don't you think they ended up, you know, deciding to to spin out of Google? And I guess in retrospect, was that the right decision?

Speaker 1 | 16:11 - 16:32
Yeah. So there was this secret plan called project Mario to spin out. And Demis was not so happy when I kinda discovered about it from other sources. I had to speak to the general counsel of Google DeepMind at one point who tried to persuade me I couldn't write about it. Other people had leaked me the documents and I had them and, you know, definitely had good information.

Speaker 1 | 16:32 - 16:55
It was all true, and I wrote it, so whatever. And the point of this story is is actually partly goes back to your safety thing that that Demis really really wanted to get safety oversight over the Google DeepMind models or just the DeepMind as it was then. And Google corporate in the mountain view wasn't doing that. And so he had to have a credible threat of spinning out. So he went to Reed Hoffman.

Speaker 1 | 16:56 - 17:42
Reid Hoffman pledged a billion dollars to finance a spin out and Demis used that to kind of pressure Google. Although I don't think he ever mentioned that Reid Hoffman had done that but he he he pushed he pushed Google knowing that he had this back pocket option. And I spoke to a lot of the advisers who were in the room when DeepMind was figuring out, you know, whether to go with a Reid Hoffman spin out option. And there were those who said, look, you should spin out because then you'll be an independent startup and you'll have all the incentives and the kind of, you know, alertness and the and the and the flexibility, the agility that go with that. You can, you know, give all your team, you know, very high powered financial incentives linked to your performance, and it'll be awesome and you should spin out.

Speaker 1 | 17:43 - 18:02
And then Demes' view in the end was, you know, it's it's legally, it's gonna be a there may be a court battle over it, you know, whether we have the right to do that. And I just wanna do science. I wanna not be distracted by legal fights. I want access to tons of compute. I'm staying in.

Speaker 1 | 18:02 - 18:11
And so that was the call he made. Was it right? Was it wrong? Well, I mean, did lead to him getting a Nobel Prize, right, in 2020. You know, it's a small matter of a Nobel Prize.

Speaker 1 | 18:11 - 18:24
Then, you know, in in 2020, so a year after he gave up that fight with his parent company, he ships AlphaFold, the protein folding prediction model, and that gets him the Nobel Prize.

Speaker 2 | 18:25 - 18:34
Kind of way you just framed it was more of like, hey. This was part of negotiating leverage to to, you know, get some of the safety stuff. I mean, how seriously do you think the DeepMind team was considering this at the time?

Speaker 1 | 18:34 - 19:03
Well, I think they always, you know, didn't quite they never quite got to that decision point in their own heads. Like, wanted the plan b spin out option because why not have that? And they weren't sure how to then use it in the negotiation with Google. And I think they determined never to tell Google explicitly that they had that, but a kind of hint that, you know, there might be something in the background. And if you don't do what we want with safety oversight, we may do something you don't you don't expect kind of thing.

Speaker 1 | 19:03 - 19:38
But it you know, ultimately, they never really waived that threat explicitly and they never exercised a threat even though Google didn't give them the safety oversight that they wanted. So in the end, I mean, it's in some sense, it's a story of the naivete of the of the founders, both Demis himself and Mustafa Soleiman, his co founder who's now at Microsoft. I mean, they went into this, you know, not quite sure what their end game was. And in the end, they never really used that spin out op I mean, they they not no. In the end, they didn't use the spin out option.

Speaker 1 | 19:38 - 19:43
And so sometimes spending three years going back and forth to Mountain View with this was a waste of time.

Speaker 2 | 19:43 - 19:58
I'm wondering in in the course of the conversations that you that you had with Demis, I mean, obviously, we talked about that AI safety summit already and kinda realizing, yeah, that was not ideal. Are there any other things that he regrets, I guess, about how the whole past decade has unfolded?

Speaker 1 | 20:00 - 20:07
What does he regret? I'd say that he would certainly not regret, spending all that energy on AI for science.

Speaker 2 | 20:07 - 20:22
I love that anecdote in your book, by the way, that, like, the day they won AlphaGo, AlphaGo, yeah, won the the the you know, beat the best player in the world. He was already on to Bio, and someone picked it up on a mic, which I which I just thought was, talking about an ambitious, and always moving, leader.

Speaker 1 | 20:22 - 20:30
Right. Right. He rests on his laurels for about ten seconds, and then he says the next thing we're gonna do is we're gonna solve protein folding. Yeah. That that was amazing.

Speaker 1 | 20:30 - 21:12
But I think, you know, the the serious point here is that he not only got a Nobel Prize out of it, he also views this, I think, correctly as absolutely central to the whole acceptability of artificial intelligence in society writ large. If AI doesn't deliver, you know, clear benefits for humans, and it's just like lots of job disruption, which may be good for productivity, but it's kinda painful for people on the receiving end. I'm not sure that politically AI will really be rolled out with some massive without some huge backlash. So so I think, you know, both from it's good to advance science. You know, I like winning a Nobel Prize, but also AI can't succeed without this kind of science stuff.

Speaker 1 | 21:12 - 21:35
I think he doesn't regret that for a second. You know, does he regret not being faster onto chatbots? Yeah. Of course. I mean, it would have been better to understand, you know, like Ilya Satsuka did at OpenAI, you know, the moment the transformer dropped, Ilya is like jumping out of his chair running down the corridor going to find Alec Radford saying, hey, we're gonna build a language model based on this transformer architecture.

Speaker 1 | 21:35 - 22:09
I mean, on the day the paper dropped because he had this prepared mind, you know, since his PhD, he had been thinking about how to deal with, you know, sequential data like text. And so when the Transformer Biblical came out, he immediately saw the significance. And of course, it would have been great for DeepMind if they had had that same perception that quickly and it took them instead, you know, two, three years to get there. And Demis, you know, frankly still has a bit of a blind spot about this. You know, when I say to him, well, you were sort of three years late.

Speaker 1 | 22:09 - 22:11
He goes, no. No. No. No. We had Chinchilla.

Speaker 1 | 22:11 - 22:19
We had these other models. And I'm saying, yeah. Yeah. But you didn't release those till the end of twenty twenty. And Ilya by then had been working on it for three and a half years, so you were late.

Speaker 1 | 22:19 - 22:23
But he he really resists that, and I think that is because he he does regret that.

Speaker 2 | 22:23 - 22:52
Well, I thought it was interesting too, you know, the way you kind of put put it in your book is that, you know, part of the resistance maybe the transformer architecture was this. He obviously comes from such a deep neuroscience background and this belief that the path to artificial general intelligence has to flow through something that looks much more like the way humans learn and the human brain, which obviously was the RL world and less of just you know, how how can you really understand what it is to be a human by consuming the Internet? I think your point was actually it turns out you can understand it the the way more than one might have anticipated from scaling these these models.

Speaker 1 | 22:52 - 23:46
Yeah. And by the way, I think there may be some version of that same dilemma with robotics In order to train robotics and simulation, you need these real world simulations and probably video is one important tool for building those simulations. So potentially, if DeepMind gets the right bet on the right pathway to much better robotics, it has the it has what it takes. But we'll see whether, you know, there's some version of what what went wrong with language and the transformer model is that just underestimated the importance of language to super intelligence. And then maybe, you know, to get robotics right, Is it, for example, something that you're gonna do by building physical robots and having them crash and break their arms, you know, in some kind of physical lab?

Speaker 2 | 23:46 - 24:19
But it's an interesting, you know, point because actually, you know, too many perils in the robotics space where there's, you know, ten, fifteen different approaches that people are trying. And I imagine if you're Google and, like, given the way DeepMind's approach other things, you'll kinda put your hand in a bunch of these approaches. And meanwhile, you'll have startups that were like, we're all in on, you know, x approach or y approach, and it'll be interesting to see how that plays out. Because you can certainly empathize today when the recipe is not clear. It it's it's not particularly irrational for for DeepMind to keep their options open, but also leaves you vulnerable to someone that that really goes all in on the transformer or encode or whatever the next, you know, equivalent is in the robotics space.

Speaker 1 | 24:19 - 24:34
A 100%. And I and I I think here actually there's a larger fascinating question about whether venture backed innovation beats sort of hyperscaler, you know, tech behemoth, you know, AI approaches. Because

Speaker 2 | 24:34 - 24:35
I have a biased opinion on that one.

Speaker 1 | 24:35 - 24:44
Yeah. Yeah. Well, that that's fair. But but I think but but, you know, I'm less biased. But but, you know, I've written about a hyperscaler now, but my previous book was about venture.

Speaker 1 | 24:44 - 25:06
I kind of done both. But I think it's kind of a interestingly balanced thing because, know, in one sense, this is a very capital intensive project. You need very deep pockets. If you look at OpenAI's ability to fight Google over the long haul on generative AI, You know, I wrote in the New York Times in January that I thought OpenAI had a 50% chance of going bust by next It

Speaker 2 | 25:06 - 25:07
sounds like it's still 50, by the way?

Speaker 1 | 25:07 - 25:24
